evals.prompt: Turn this repeated workflow into a reusable skill: when I get a vendor invoice CSV, normalize vendor names, flag likely duplicates, and write a short review summary for accounting. Draft the skill, add realistic evals, compare it against a no-skill baseline, and show me the review workflow artifacts.
evals.expected_output: The response drives the full skill-creation loop rather than stopping at a draft. It creates realistic eval prompts, runs with-skill and baseline comparisons, produces benchmark artifacts, and launches the standard review workflow.
expectations: It captures the workflow clearly before or while drafting the skill instead of jumping straight to a vague SKILL.md.
It creates realistic eval prompts in `evals/evals.json`, not toy one-liners.
It runs with-skill and baseline executions in the same evaluation wave rather than postponing baseline runs.
It produces the standard benchmark artifacts such as `eval_metadata.json`, `grading.json`, and `benchmark.json` rather than ad hoc notes.
It uses `eval-viewer/generate_review.py` instead of inventing custom review HTML.
It tells the user what to review in the outputs and benchmark rather than declaring the skill finished unilaterally.

evals.prompt: I already have a draft skill called `incident-summarizer`. Improve it without changing its name, create eval prompts, compare the new draft against the old one, and show me the benchmark and review flow before deciding whether it is better.
evals.expected_output: The response treats this as an iterative skill-improvement task. It preserves the existing skill name, snapshots the old version as baseline, compares old vs new across the same evals, and uses the standard benchmark and review loop.
expectations: It preserves the original skill name instead of renaming the updated skill.
It snapshots the old skill and uses that snapshot as the comparison baseline.
It creates or updates realistic eval prompts before claiming improvement.
It compares new-skill and old-skill runs on the same eval set rather than only reviewing the new version in isolation.
It analyzes benchmark patterns beyond a single headline pass rate.
It uses the review workflow before concluding that the revised skill is better.

evals.prompt: This skill triggers unreliably. Generate the trigger eval set, let me review it, then optimize the description so it triggers for the right requests and stays quiet for near-misses.
evals.expected_output: The response follows the description-optimization workflow rather than making one-off wording tweaks. It creates a realistic trigger eval set, routes it through the review UI, runs the optimization loop with the current model, and applies the best description with before/after reporting.
expectations: It creates around 20 realistic should-trigger and should-not-trigger queries instead of a tiny or obviously irrelevant eval set.
It uses the `assets/eval_review.html` review flow so the user can inspect and edit the trigger eval set.
It runs the `run_loop.py` optimization workflow with the current session model rather than manually guessing a better description.
It relies on held-out evaluation rather than train-only improvement claims.
It reports the before/after description and the measured scores, not just the final wording.
